title: Unit 19 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Voices, Moods & Special Forms

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 19: Voices, Moods and Special Forms. We have come a long way. We have learned how to act... how to be acted upon... and how to command. In this unit... we bring it all together. I'm William... and here with me is Athina. Athina... what is the fundamental distinction between the Active and Passive Voice?
Γεια σας!
In Greek, the line is very clear. Active is when you do it. Passive is when it’s done to you... or when you do it to yourself!
Εγώ πλένω το αυτοκίνητο... ή εγώ πλένομαι.
I wash the car... or I wash myself. The ending changes completely.
And the 'Moods'? In English, we mostly talk about moods when we're grumpy. But in Greek, they are settings for reality. Athina, what about the negation rules?
This is vital! In the Indicative mood... for simple facts... we use one word. For the Subjunctive and Imperative... for desires and commands... we use another.
Δεν... Μην.
Never mix them up!
Exactly. Fact equals
Δεν.
Desire or Command equals
Μην.
Now, let’s talk about a special 'Action' form... the Gerund. In English, it’s the '-ing' ending. I came running. Athina... how do we say that?
We use the endings
-οντας ή -ώντας.
Ήρθε τρέχοντας... έφυγε γελώντας.
He came running... he left laughing. It’s an adverbial form, so it never changes for person or number. It’s very stable.
Zero declension! That’s music to my ears. Finally, the big question. Does Greek have a true infinitive? Like 'to be' or 'to run'?
No! Common Greek lost the infinitive centuries ago. We use 'Na' + the verb. The only tiny remnant is the one we use for the Perfect tenses.
Έχω γράψει.
I have written. Outside of that... the infinitive is dead.

Fascinating. This unit is the master overview. You now see the whole map. You aren't just memorizing endings anymore... you are choosing rooms and lenses. Excellent work... we'll see you in the next unit.
Γεια σας!
